You notice whose wording is praised as “practical” and whose wording is described as “rigid” or “unhelpful.” You see that if you write plainly, suddenly people become concerned about your attitude, your flexibility, your understanding of context. Bas if you soften everything into administrative mist, then you are seen as someone who understands the institution.

Did I feel tempted? Aiwa, I would be dishonest if I said no. Not because I stopped seeing the truth, but because constant friction exhausts a person. Sometimes you think: let me reduce the sharpness this one time, let me save my energy, let me not trigger the same useless reaction again. This is how people get absorbed, I think—not always through corruption in the dramatic sense, but through fatigue. Through learning that survival has its own vocabulary.

What saved me, at least partly, was that I could not stop seeing the person at the far end of the sentence. The resident, the street user, the family that inherits the inconvenience long after the meeting is forgotten. So when they asked for “balance,” I began asking myself: balance for whom? When they asked for “flexibility,” I thought: flexibility in relation to what—reality, or responsibility? This helped me hear the pressure more clearly
INTERVIEWER: When you resisted that pressure and kept the wording clear anyway, what was the personal cost to you inside the workplace?
SUBJECT: The personal cost was rarely dramatic in one single moment. It was more cumulative, ya‘ni, like erosion. You do not always get punished with an official penalty. Sometimes it is colder than that. You become marked. Not openly, not in a way anyone will write down, bas people begin to treat you as someone difficult to “manage.” And once this impression settles, it follows you into rooms before you even speak.

I noticed first a change in atmosphere. Conversations became more guarded around me. Some people who were relaxed before started choosing their words carefully, as if clarity from me might create risk for them. Others became politely distant. You feel this, wallah. The invitation to informal discussion becomes less frequent. Small information stops reaching you as early as it used to. And in institutions like ours, where so much depends on what is said before the official meeting, this kind of quiet distancing is not a small thing.

Then there was the reputational cost. Instead of being seen as precise, you are described as severe. Instead of principled, you are “sensitive.” Instead of responsible, you “lack pragmatism.” This is how the system defends itself—by translating moral discomfort into a personality flaw. It is very efficient. Because now the discussion is no longer about whether the wording was true; it becomes about whether you are easy to work with. And many people, frankly, care more about social smoothness than technical honesty.

There was also a career cost, or at least the sense of one. You start to understand that trust, in that environment, does not always mean trust in your competence. Sometimes it means trust that you will not create administrative inconvenience. So if you are known for writing clearly, some doors do not open with the same enthusiasm. You may not be excluded formally, la’, but you are not exactly brought closer either. You remain useful, but with caution attached to your name.

Psychologically, this was tiring. Because you begin doubting the value of repeated friction. You ask yourself: am I preserving standards, or just isolating myself while nothing changes? This is the dangerous stage. Not when a person is angry, but when he becomes tired enough to normalize what once disturbed him. I feared this more than conflict itself. In Mosul, after everything people have already endured, I did not want to become one more man who sees clearly and then teaches himself not to see.

At the same time, there was a strange kind of internal cost too: loneliness, maybe
INTERVIEWER: When that loneliness started to set in, who noticed it first in your life outside work, and what did they see in you that told them something was changing?
SUBJECT: The first to notice, I think, was my older sister. She has this way—quiet, not intrusive, bas very sharp. She does not ask many theatrical questions. She watches. She noticed before I admitted anything, even to myself. I would come home and still be physically present, but my mind was somewhere else, replaying sentences, meetings, tones of voice. Not the big events only—the small compromises, the half-phrases, the things left deliberately unclear. She saw that I was carrying the office home inside me.

What did she see? She saw narrowing. This is the best word. I became more withdrawn, more exact in an unpleasant way. Even normal conversation, I would respond as if I were cross-examining language. If someone said, “It’s fine,” I would almost instinctively ask, “Fine in what sense?” Ya‘ni, I had become suspicious not only of institutions, but of words themselves. And this is not a healthy way to live. A man cannot remain always in audit mode, wallah, or he becomes difficult even to those who love him.

She also saw fatigue that was not simple tiredness. Not just lack of sleep. More like moral exhaustion. A heaviness. I think she noticed that what disturbed me was not workload, but contamination—the feeling that if you stay too long around blurred responsibility, you begin fighting not only others but your own adaptation. She once told me, very simply, “You are talking less, but when you do talk, it is as if you are still defending yourself.” That hit me. Because it was true. I had begun speaking as if every statement would be diluted unless I protected it.

My mother noticed something too, but in a different way. Mothers read the body before the argument, you know. She saw the irritation, the shortened patience, the way I would sit in silence longer than usual. She did not frame it politically or professionally. She would just say, “Shinu, Ahmed, why is your face always carrying something?” And honestly, sometimes that simple phrasing is more accurate than analysis. My face was carrying something.

What they saw changing was not only mood. It was trust. I was losing some trust in process, in professional language, maybe even in the idea that effort inside the system naturally leads to correction. And when a person begins losing that, it leaks into how he inhabits ordinary life. He becomes harder to reassure. Less available. More inward.

Bas their noticing helped
INTERVIEWER: How did their noticing help you—did it make you open up more, or did it make you realize you needed to change something about how you were carrying all of this?
SUBJECT: Aiwa, it did both. Their noticing helped me open a door I was trying to keep closed, and at the same time it forced me to admit that the way I was carrying everything was becoming its own problem. Before that, I was telling myself, more or less, that silence was discipline. That if I kept things contained, I was being strong, professional, in control. Bas in reality, I was bringing the logic of the workplace into the house: contain, filter, compress, do not burden others. And this, ya‘ni, is not strength forever. Sometimes it is only another form of isolation.

With my sister, I opened up more gradually. Not one dramatic confession, la’. More like fragments at first. I would describe a meeting, then stop. Mention a phrase someone used, and why it bothered me more than it should. She was useful because she did not rush to solve me. She would listen and then ask one clean question, something like: “Are you angry because they were wrong, or because they made wrongness sound normal?” And these questions helped me separate things. Because the real injury was often not the mistake itself, but the normalization of it.

With my mother, it was different. I did not explain everything in technical or institutional language. There was no need. What helped there was simpler: she reminded me that if your work eats your character, then even if you remain technically correct, you are still losing something important. She would tell me to go out, visit relatives, sit with people, stop feeding only on frustration. Very ordinary advice, maybe, bas wallah it matters. Ordinary life can return proportion to you.

And yes, it made me realize I needed to change how I was carrying it. Not necessarily abandon my principles, but change the posture. I could not live permanently clenched. I had to learn that not every battle should continue inside my body after the meeting ends. This was difficult for me. Because when you care about standards, you can start believing that relaxing even internally is a kind of betrayal. But this is not sustainable. A person needs boundaries, otherwise the system occupies him rent-free, as they say.

So I started making small changes. Speaking more honestly at home instead of pretending I was merely tired. Walking alone sometimes without replaying every conversation like evidence. Limiting how much I mentally rehearsed what I should have said or what others really meant. And maybe most importantly, I began accepting that witnessing dysfunction does not require me to
INTERVIEWER: carry all of it alone. When you began accepting that, what did sharing the burden with other people actually look like in practice?
SUBJECT: Aiwa, this was an important shift for me. Before, I was carrying everything like a private file—sealed, organized, hidden. As if unless I held it alone, it would become weakness. But in practice, sharing the burden did not mean I suddenly became a man who tells everyone everything, la’, not like this. It meant I stopped acting as if endurance has to be solitary in order to be honorable.

With my sister, sharing looked like specificity. Instead of saying only, “Work is tiring,” khalas, I would say: today they changed the wording of a note in a way that removed responsibility from the decision-maker. Or: this meeting was not difficult because of disagreement, but because everyone pretended not to understand the obvious. When I spoke more precisely, the burden became more real and also more manageable. It left the vague emotional fog and became something I could examine outside myself.

With a few trusted friends, sharing looked different. Less analysis, more recognition. Sometimes we would sit, drink tea, and I would describe a situation, and immediately they would say, “Na‘am, we know this language. We have seen this also.” Wallah, there is relief in that. Not because the problem disappears, but because it stops behaving like a private madness. You realize the distortion is structural, not only in your head. This matters more than people think. A person can tolerate hardship better than unreality.

Also, I began to ask for perspective, not only sympathy. This was new for me. I would ask: am I reacting to an actual ethical issue here, or am I becoming hyper-vigilant? Is this one of the moments to push clearly, or one of the moments to document and conserve energy? Ya‘ni, I stopped assuming that all responsibility for judgment had to come only from inside me. Sometimes another person can return scale to you.

And sharing also became practical, not just emotional. For example, if I knew a certain issue was likely to become contentious, I would speak with one trusted colleague before the meeting—not to build a political camp, bas to test the language, to ask: if I phrase it this way, does it remain accurate without becoming easy to dismiss? Even this small kind of collaboration reduced the sense that every sentence was me alone against a fog.

At home, sharing looked like allowing my bad days to be visible without turning them into a performance. This is important. I did not want suffering to become identity. So sometimes it was simply saying
INTERVIEWER: When you let those bad days be visible at home without turning them into a performance, what did your family do that actually helped—and what, if anything, made it worse?
SUBJECT: What helped most was their restraint, honestly. My family did not treat every bad day as an emergency, and this was a mercy. In our houses, sometimes people love you too much in a loud way, ya‘ni, they want immediate explanation, immediate solution, immediate emotional display. But what helped me was the opposite: they made space without forcing confession.

My sister, for example, would notice my mood and adjust the tone around me without making me feel examined. She might sit near me and speak normally, not interrogate me, not say, “Tell us everything now.” This preserved dignity. It told me: we see you, bas we do not need to corner you in order to care. And when I was ready, she listened in a way that did not exaggerate my experience or reduce it. This balance is rare, wallah.

My mother helped through routine more than analysis. Food, tea, asking me to do something ordinary, telling me to visit an uncle, to get out of my own head a little. Some people think this is avoidance, but not always. Sometimes ordinary gestures are what keep a person tied to life when his mind is becoming too abstract, too full of institutional poison. Her care reminded me that I was still a son, a brother, not only a man in conflict with a system.

What also helped was that they did not immediately turn my frustration into a morality play. They did not say, “You are too good for this world,” or “Everyone else is corrupt and only you are clean.” La’, and alhamdulillah for that. Such talk sounds supportive, bas it is dangerous. It isolates you further and feeds ego at the same time. I needed grounding, not sainthood.

As for what made it worse—yes, there were things. Sometimes advice came too quickly. “Ignore it.” “Do your job and go home.” “Why do you care so much?” These phrases, even when well-meant, can make a person feel more alone, because they simplify what is not simple. If detachment were so easy, I would have done it already. Also, on some days, too much sympathy made it worse. If someone looked at me with excessive worry, as if I were breaking apart, I would immediately close again. I did not want to become an object of concern inside my own house.

And there is another thing: comparisons. In a place like Mosul, people have survived war, displacement, loss—real devastation. So
INTERVIEWER: When people compared your work stress to the much bigger suffering others had lived through in Mosul, how did that affect your willingness to speak honestly about what you were carrying?
SUBJECT: It made me more hesitant, of course. In Mosul, a person develops an internal censor very early. You tell yourself: who am I to complain? People buried family, lost homes, started life again from zero. So compared to that, what is moral fatigue in an office? What is distrust, what is this slow corrosion? It can seem small, almost shameful to name.

Bas this comparison is not always wise. It can produce humility, نعم, but it can also produce silence that is too absolute. Suffering does not need competition in order to be real. The fact that others endured catastrophe does not mean the smaller injuries are harmless. Sometimes the quieter forms of damage are dangerous precisely because they are easy to dismiss. They do not explode your life; they narrow it. Gradually. They make you less open, less trusting, more divided inside yourself. This is not tragedy in the dramatic sense, la’, but it still matters.

So when people made those comparisons carelessly, I spoke less honestly. I would edit myself. I would reduce everything to “just tired” or “just work pressure,” because I did not want to sound indulgent. And once you begin doing that repeatedly, you become less able to describe your own condition even to yourself. Language shrinks. You stop saying, “This is affecting my character,” and say only, “It’s nothing.” Wallah, “it’s nothing” can become its own kind of lie.

At the same time, living in Mosul gave me another perspective too. It taught me that endurance is not only for dramatic suffering. People here continue through all kinds of burdens—grief, bureaucracy, disappointment, broken trust, all of it. So I began to think: maybe honesty is not disrespectful to larger suffering. Maybe it is part of resisting further damage. If you name what is happening to you without exaggeration, this is not self-pity. It is discipline of another kind.

What helped was when someone understood the distinction and said, in effect: yes, others suffered more, but that is not a command for you to become mute. Then I could speak more freely. Not as a victim among victims, ya‘ni, but simply as a man trying not to let one kind of pressure deform the rest of his life.
INTERVIEWER: When you finally did speak more freely in that way, who was the first person outside your family you trusted with it—and what made you decide they were safe enough to hear the unedited version?
SUBJECT: The first person outside my family was an old friend from university, also an engineer, though he went more into site work while I stayed closer to the institutional side. I chose him very deliberately, not randomly. In our culture, and especially in a city like Mosul, trust is not built from who is simply kind. Kindness is good, of course, bas it is not enough. I needed someone who understood systems, hierarchy, paperwork, the theater of professionalism—someone who would recognize the difference between ordinary work frustration and the deeper exhaustion that comes when language itself becomes compromised.

What made him feel safe was his manner over many years. He was not a man who rushed to conclusions, and not a man who collected other people’s private words for social use later. This is important, wallah. Some people listen only so they can convert your pain into a story, or into advice, or into evidence of their own wisdom. He did not do this. He had a kind of disciplined attention. When you spoke, he did not interrupt to perform understanding.

Also, I had watched how he handled ambiguity in his own life. He could admit confusion without becoming dramatic. He could criticize something without turning himself into a martyr. This gave me confidence. Because I did not want to speak to someone who would immediately say, “Aiwa, you must resign,” or “All of them are corrupt,” or “You are carrying the nation on your shoulders.” La’. I needed a person who could tolerate complexity. Someone who would let the matter remain human-sized, even if it was morally heavy.

The moment itself was not ceremonial. We were sitting in a café, late, and the conversation moved from ordinary things into work. At first I spoke in the edited version, the acceptable version. Then he asked me one question that made me change course. He said, more or less: “Are you tired from the workload, or from what you have to pretend not to notice?” This was a precise question. Too precise. It told me he already understood the category of problem. After that, I spoke more openly.

And by “unedited,” I do not mean I poured everything out like a flood. Even then, I was measured. Bas I stopped protecting the situation from accurate language. I said that what was draining me was not inefficiency alone, but the normalization of evasiveness. That I felt myself becoming internally split—one part doing the work, another part constantly monitoring what was being distorted, softened, hidden. I
INTERVIEWER: What did he say when you told him that—that you felt internally split between doing the work and constantly tracking what was being softened or hidden?
SUBJECT: He was quiet first. This, in itself, helped me. He did not jump in with slogans, did not say, “Aiwa, exactly, this country is finished,” and he did not rush to comfort me with empty كلام. He let the sentence stay in the air for a moment, as if to give it its proper weight.

Then he said something like: “Na‘am. This is expensive.” Very simple. But he meant expensive in the inner sense, ya‘ni—not money, not even only career. Expensive to the mind, to the nerves, to your sense of your own coherence. He said when a man must keep translating reality into acceptable language every day, after some time he no longer knows whether he is being diplomatic or becoming altered. Wallah, this struck me hard because it was exactly the fear. Not that I would face one dramatic collapse, la’, but that I would slowly adapt in ways I could not easily reverse.

He also told me not to flatter myself about it. And strangely, this also helped. He said: “Be careful. Sometimes we describe ourselves as morally exhausted when in fact we are becoming morally vain.” Meaning, there is a danger in loving one’s own disappointment too much, in turning disillusionment into identity. In formal Arabic I would say: كان يحذرني من تحويل النزاهة إلى مسرح داخلي. He wanted me to remain honest without becoming self-important. This was important medicine, honestly.

After that, he asked practical questions. Not dramatic ones. He said: where exactly do you feel the split most—meetings, written notes, verbal instructions, approvals? Is it constant, or are there specific people or situations that trigger it? Are you still able to say one clear sentence in the room when something matters, or have you moved entirely into private resentment? This line of questioning grounded me. It turned the problem from fog into structure.

And then he said something I still remember: “If you cannot change the whole climate, at least do not let it colonize your tongue.” Ya‘ni, maybe I could not reform the institution, maybe not even my department, bas I could still refuse certain internal concessions. I could choose accuracy where possible. I could document clearly. I could avoid participating in falsification even when I could not defeat it. He was not speaking like a hero from television, you understand. He knew limits. But he was trying to protect a smaller, more realistic form of integrity.

He also told me
INTERVIEWER: What did he tell you after that about your limits—how to protect your integrity without letting the whole struggle consume you?
SUBJECT: He told me, very calmly, that limits are not betrayal. This was important for me to hear because, frankly, I had begun to think in very absolute ways: either I resist everything or I become part of it. Bas life, especially here, is not lived in these clean categories. He said: “If you try to carry every distortion personally, you will not become more principled—you will only become unusable.” This word, unusable, stayed with me. لأنه صحيح. A man can burn through himself and then imagine this is integrity, when in fact it is only exhaustion with better marketing.

He advised me to distinguish between what I must refuse, what I should document, and what I simply need to witness without absorbing into my bloodstream. In formal Arabic, ya‘ni, he was asking for tamyīz—discernment, not passivity. There are things that, if you participate in them, they damage your character directly. These require a boundary, even if it costs something. Then there are things you may not be able to stop, bas you can at least name them accurately in your own mind, record them properly, and not decorate them with lies. And then there is a third category: the daily nonsense, the petty theater, the inflated talk, the small bureaucratic humiliations. If you swallow all of this as if each item were a moral emergency, you will lose proportion.

He said also: choose your points of friction. Do not argue at every meeting just to prove to yourself that you are still awake. This hit me, wallah, because sometimes a person begins performing resistance for himself. He wants evidence that he has not surrendered, so he objects constantly—even when the objection changes nothing and only feeds his own agitation. My friend told me: “Save your clarity for the moments that matter. Otherwise even your true objections will start sounding like habit.”

Another thing he said was to keep one part of life deliberately outside the institution. Not only physically outside, but spiritually outside, if I can put it that way. He asked me, “When was the last time you did something not organized by frustration?” A harsh question, bas fair. Because if all your reading, all your conversations, all your evenings become reactions to corruption or failure, then the system has already occupied more of you than your job description. So he pushed me toward ordinary anchors—friends, family visits, exercise, even silly things, honestly. Tea in the evening, football talk, helping someone with a
INTERVIEWER: What happened when you actually tried to follow that advice—especially the part about choosing your points of friction instead of objecting to everything?
SUBJECT: What happened? In truth, it made me less noisy and more effective—bas not immediately. At first, reducing my objections felt almost like cowardice. I had become accustomed to a certain internal posture where constant irritation served as proof, to myself, that I had not gone numb. So when I began choosing my points of friction more carefully, there was an uncomfortable period where I thought: maybe I am simply becoming softer, more accommodating, more like the people I criticize. This suspicion followed me for some time.

لكن gradually I saw the difference between permanent agitation and actual integrity. Before, I was reacting to too many things at once. Every meeting carried ten small provocations, five evasions, some inflated language, some avoidance of responsibility. If I treated each one as requiring intervention, then two things happened: first, I exhausted myself; second, people stopped hearing distinction in my voice. My objections began to blend together. Even when I was right, it sounded to others like I was merely predictable. In colloquial terms, ya‘ni, I became “the man who always has a problem.” This is not a useful role if you want your words to retain force.

So I started asking myself, sometimes even silently during the meeting: is this distortion cosmetic, or is it consequential? Is this merely annoying, or does it create a false record, transfer blame unfairly, hide a risk, or set a bad precedent? That small internal filter changed a lot. It allowed me to conserve energy for the moments where precision mattered. For example, if someone used exaggerated language to praise a weak plan, maybe I let that pass. Bas if the same meeting moved toward documenting something inaccurately, or implying that a technical concern had been resolved when it had not, then I would speak. More briefly than before, and more concretely.

And I noticed something important: when I spoke less often, people listened more carefully. Not because the environment became healthy—la’, let us not romanticize it—but because my interventions no longer felt ambient. They had shape. If I said, “This wording is not accurate,” or “We cannot record completion when the site condition remains unresolved,” it landed differently. There was less theatrical frustration around it, more discipline. Even those who did not like it had to recognize that I was not objecting for sport.

Of course, there was a cost too. Choosing one point means letting another pass, and sometimes afterward I would go home replaying the meeting, asking myself whether I had exercised wisdom
INTERVIEWER: When you went home replaying those meetings, what was the hardest kind of thing to let pass without feeling like you were betraying your own standards?
SUBJECT: The hardest thing to let pass was not the big obvious corruption, strangely. Those cases, at least internally, are clear. You know what they are. You know why they matter. The more difficult thing was the small falsifications that present themselves as practicality. This is where the soul gets tired, ya‘ni. Not when someone says something openly rotten, but when a room quietly agrees to describe a failure as a delay, or a risk as a manageable complication, or an absence of decision as ongoing coordination. هذه اللغة المتعبة. This language exhausts a person because it asks you to cooperate without ever announcing the terms.

When I let that pass, even for reasons of strategy, I would sometimes feel contaminated by it. Not because I had endorsed it exactly, bas because I had remained present while reality was being trimmed into something more convenient. And afterward at home I would ask myself: did I show discipline, or did I borrow the system’s vocabulary for one hour and call it maturity? This was the sharper question.

Also difficult was when blame was being redistributed in subtle ways. I mean those moments when nobody tells a direct lie, la’, but the sequence of events is narrated so that responsibility becomes foggy. A technical warning was raised, but now it is being treated as if it came too late. A decision was imposed verbally, but now on paper it appears collective. A deadline was unrealistic from the beginning, but later the problem is described as poor follow-through. These things disturbed me deeply because they injure truth by administration, not by drama. And if you interrupt every such moment, you become impossible. If you interrupt none of them, you become complicit. So this was the narrow bridge.

There was another category too: the humiliation of standards through tone. Sometimes no single sentence was false enough to justify a confrontation, bas the whole atmosphere encouraged carelessness—technical matters treated as inconveniences, caution spoken of like weakness, precision made to feel socially awkward. Wallah, this could affect me more than a direct argument. Because then the pressure is not only on what you say, but on what kind of person you are allowed to be in the room. To insist on accuracy starts to make you seem rigid, difficult, unable to “move things forward.” This is a very efficient form of moral pressure.

What made it painful was that my standards were not heroic standards. I was not asking for purity, not imagining I could cleanse an institution with one principled sentence. La’. I only wanted not to become
INTERVIEWER: When you say, “I only wanted not to become—,” how would you finish that sentence now?
SUBJECT: I would finish it like this: I only wanted not to become internally divided to the point that I could no longer recognize my own voice.

Ya‘ni, not a hero, not a martyr, not some dramatic figure of refusal. بس simply a man who can still say a true sentence and feel that it belongs to him. This was the fear from the beginning—that adaptation would not arrive as one shameful event, but as a slow education in acceptable distortion. A word here, a silence there, one compromise explained as necessity, another as timing, until finally you are functioning well, perhaps even respected, but inwardly borrowed. وهذا هو الخراب الحقيقي، in my view.

Coming from Mosul, and with my background in civil engineering, I was trained to think that structure matters. If the foundation is compromised, the crack may not appear immediately, bas it is already there. I began to see something similar in a person. You can continue, you can perform competence, you can submit reports, attend meetings, smile even—لكن if your language is no longer connected to your judgment, then something structural in you has started to fail.

So now, if I finish that sentence honestly, I would say: I only wanted not to become fluent in my own diminishment. Not to become one of those men who explain away every erosion as experience, every silence as wisdom, every surrender as realism. There is a kind of adulthood here that people praise too quickly—being “practical,” being “flexible,” knowing how things are done. Sometimes this is maturity, yes. Bas sometimes it is only defeat with polished manners.

And also—if I speak plainly—I did not want bitterness to become my whole personality. Because that too is a deformation. You resist corruption outside and meanwhile something harsh colonizes you from within. Then you are truthful maybe, but no longer generous, no longer capable of ordinary life, no longer good company even for the people you love. This is not integrity either. هذا أيضا نوع من الخسارة.

So the sentence ends there, perhaps: I wanted to remain legible to myself. To keep some inner alignment between what I saw, what I said, and what I could live with afterward when I went home and sat alone with tea and silence. If a man loses even that, شنو يبقى له؟
INTERVIEWER: When you think about staying “legible” to yourself like that, can you remember a specific moment when you went home, sat with that tea and silence, and felt you had either protected that inner alignment—or failed it?
SUBJECT: Yes. I remember one evening very clearly, and wallah it stayed with me because it was not a dramatic scandal, not some cinematic confrontation. It was exactly the kind of ordinary administrative moment that tests a person more than the obvious disasters.

There had been a discussion around a site issue—nothing abstract, something practical, measurable. A concern had been raised earlier about conditions not being ready, and the warning was real, bas inconvenient. Later, in the meeting, the language began to shift. Not into a direct lie, no. That would almost be easier. Instead the wording moved toward presenting the matter as if the obstacle had been noted, managed, and essentially absorbed into normal process. A nice smooth sentence, very professional on the surface. But the sentence was false in effect. It would have created a record cleaner than reality.

I remember sitting there and feeling that familiar split beginning inside me. Part of me said: leave it. Everyone understands what this is. If you interrupt now, you will turn a manageable meeting into another episode. You will become, again, the difficult one. Bas another part of me said: if this wording enters the record, then later the facts themselves will become impolite. That is how it happens. First the language is adjusted, then memory follows it.

So I waited a little—too long maybe—and then I spoke. Not emotionally. This was the difference, I think. Before, I might have spoken with accumulated irritation from ten previous things. That day I forced myself to be narrow and exact. I said, more or less: this phrasing suggests resolution that has not actually occurred; if we keep it, the record will not reflect the site condition accurately. Let us either state the issue as pending, or specify what remains unresolved. Simple. Technical. Hard to romanticize.

The room became slightly cold, you know? Not explosive. Just that small tightening people do when you refuse the convenient sentence. Someone tried to soften it—said we should not complicate the wording unnecessarily, that everyone already understood the context. وهذه الجملة معروفة، of course. “Everyone understands.” Meaning: let us preserve ambiguity because it is socially useful. I repeated myself, shorter this time. I said accuracy here is not complication.

In the end the wording was adjusted, though not perfectly. A partial correction, let us say. I did not win some great moral victory. Nobody looked at me with admiration. Probably some were annoyed. One person afterward spoke to me in that polite tone people use
INTERVIEWER: What was in that polite tone afterward—and when you went home that night, did you feel more at peace for speaking up, or more exhausted by the cost of it?
SUBJECT: In that polite tone there was a warning disguised as professional advice. You know the type. Very calm, very reasonable, almost generous on the surface. He said something like: yes, your point was technically fair, but these things can be handled with a lighter touch; not every correction needs to be pressed in the room; sometimes precision must serve process, not obstruct it. كلام مرتب، clean language, no insult, nothing you can point to and say: here, this is the offense. Bas the message was clear: do not make accuracy socially expensive.

And honestly, this kind of tone can trouble me more than open hostility. Open hostility is simple—you know where you stand. But this polished managerial manner tries to recruit your own self-doubt against you. It invites you to wonder whether your insistence is really integrity, or just stiffness... whether you are defending truth, or merely attached to being right. وهذا هو الفخ. Because the criticism is not absurd; it contains enough plausibility to enter your head and sit there.

So when I went home that night, I felt both things—more at peace and more exhausted. At peace because the sentence in the record had not passed unchallenged. However small the correction, I had not collaborated with the erasure. I could sit with tea and say: at least today, my voice did not work against my own judgment. This matters to me more than I can easily explain. These small moments accumulate. A man is built from them, or hollowed out by them.

But I was also exhausted, yes. Not because speaking took so much energy in itself, bas because of the atmosphere around it—the tiny chill in the room, the implication that one has violated some unwritten etiquette by refusing a useful ambiguity. It is tiring to pay that social price again and again for matters that should be ordinary. Accuracy should not feel like sabotage. Yet often it does.

What stayed with me that night was not pride. More like a stern kind of relief. I had protected something, but only partially, and at a cost that was never visible on paper. No one writes in the minutes: one man spent the evening recovering from ten seconds of saying the obvious. But this is sometimes the real labor, ya‘ni—not the technical point itself, but resisting the soft pressure to become casual with reality.

So yes, I was more at peace than if I had stayed silent. La’ shak. But peace is not the same as lightness. I slept with a quieter conscience
INTERVIEWER: When you say you slept with a quieter conscience but not with lightness, what did the next morning feel like when you had to walk back into the same workplace again?
SUBJECT: The next morning felt heavy, بصراحة. Not dramatic fear, not like in films where a man walks into battle. La’. It was more ordinary than that, and maybe because it was ordinary it was harder. I woke up with that familiar weight in the chest—the knowledge that nothing had fundamentally changed. The meeting was over, yes, the wording was adjusted a little, yes, but the culture remained the culture. And I had to re-enter it as the same person, carrying the same name, the same reputation: technically solid maybe, but “difficult” if necessary. وهذه التسمية تتعب.

What I remember is that I became very alert to small things. The greetings in the corridor. The tone of “sabah el-khair.” Whether someone’s friendliness had cooled by five percent. Whether a colleague was now joking with me a little more carefully, or excluding me from some side conversation, or overcompensating with politeness. This is the ugly part, ya‘ni—not persecution, not some grand punishment, bas these tiny atmospheric shifts that make you feel the social invoice has been delivered.

At the same time, there was also a thin line of steadiness in me. Because once you have said the true thing, even imperfectly, the next morning you do not need to rehearse excuses for your own silence. This gives a person a certain internal quiet. Not happiness, exactly. More like structural stability. As an engineer, I think of it this way: the load is still there, the stresses are still there, but at least the member has not been asked to carry forces it was never meant to carry. The distortion is less.

Still, I would be lying if I said I walked in nobly. La’, wallah. Part of me would already be calculating: do I speak less today? Do I make myself easier in other ways so the system does not keep reacting against me? Do I smile more? Do I choose one battle and let three pass? This is the fatigue—not only resisting outside pressure, but monitoring the internal negotiations that begin before you even sit at your desk.

And there is another feeling too: a kind of sadness. Because you realize that what should have been a normal professional correction has acquired moral weight in your life. This is not healthy for an institution. A workplace should not force a man to derive dignity from simply insisting that a record reflect reality. Bas when standards are weak, ordinary decency starts to feel like an act of resistance. That morning
INTERVIEWER: That morning, when you felt yourself already calculating how much to speak, soften, or hold back, what did you actually choose to do differently once you sat down at your desk?
SUBJECT: That morning, I chose discipline more than defiance. يعني I did not come in with the mood of proving something. I knew if I entered the day carrying last night on my face, every interaction would become heavier than it needed to be. So first, I made myself do the ordinary things properly. I greeted people normally, without coldness and without extra performance. I answered emails carefully. I reviewed the pending technical notes in front of me and tried to return the day to its real scale. Sometimes this is necessary, bas not to let one incident expand until it colonizes the whole atmosphere.

But yes, I also adjusted something. I became more deliberate with speech. Less spontaneous. I remember that clearly. Instead of offering observations as they came to me, I began filtering: is this point essential, or is it only correct? There is a difference. Before, I might have insisted on every imprecision because each one bothered me. That morning I decided I could not live like that every hour. A man will burn out. So I told myself: reserve force for what alters decisions, records, safety, responsibility. Do not spend your whole soul on vanity corrections.

At the same time, I was careful not to overcorrect into silence. وهذا مهم. Because after a tense moment, sometimes a person reacts by becoming too agreeable, as if to repair the social air. I felt that temptation. To be easier, lighter, less exact, just for one day. But I knew that if I started compensating in that way, I would resent myself by evening. So what I did was this: I spoke when the matter touched substance, and when I spoke, I kept it narrow. No commentary, no extra moral charge, no visible frustration. Just the point itself.

There was also a small practical thing I did, and maybe it sounds minor, but for me it mattered. I wrote more down. Not in a dramatic way, not building a secret archive like some suspicious man. La’. Simply because when the environment becomes slippery, memory alone is not enough. So I documented my technical notes more cleanly, dated things properly, clarified pending items in writing. This gave me a little protection internally. It meant I did not have to carry every unresolved issue inside my body.

Emotionally, I tried to lower my expectation of the workplace as a moral space. وهذه كانت مؤلمة شويّة. Because part of my exhaustion came from still hoping that if one spoke clearly enough, others would meet clarity with respect. Sometimes they
INTERVIEWER: When you started lowering that expectation — accepting that the workplace might not meet clarity with respect — what did that change in the way you judged the people around you?
SUBJECT: It made me judge them with less heat, but maybe with more sadness. يعني before, when someone chose ambiguity over clarity, I would feel almost personally offended by it, as if each compromise was a direct betrayal of the profession, of responsibility, even of basic dignity. And maybe sometimes it was. Bas when I lowered the expectation, I started seeing that many people were not evil, not even especially dishonest in the dramatic sense. They were adapted. This is different.

Some had simply learned how to survive inside a weak culture. They had made a settlement with it years before. They knew which sentence to leave vague, which issue to postpone, which truth to say privately but not in the room. They were not always defending falsehood because they loved falsehood. Often they were defending continuity—salary, relations, position, calm. A kind of daily stability. And to be fair, a man understands this. خصوصاً in our countries, where institutions do not carry people gently. Everyone is tired, everyone has family, everyone is managing ten pressures at once. So I became slower to turn every compromise into a moral indictment of the person.

But this did not mean I respected the behavior more. La’, not really. It only meant I stopped being shocked by it every time. I began to separate the human being from the habit he had submitted to. One can feel sympathy and still make a hard judgment. This colleague may be decent in private, generous even, but professionally he has allowed himself to become soft where he should be firm. Another may speak beautifully about principles over tea, bas in the meeting he follows the safer wind. So my judgments became more granular, less theatrical.

And there was an uncomfortable mirror in this also. Because once you see that others are “adapted,” you must ask yourself whether you are becoming adapted too—just in a more elegant language. هذا السؤال يخوف. If I say, “they are only surviving,” then one day I may use the same excuse for myself. So lowering the expectation helped me reduce anger, yes, but it also forced me to be more vigilant. Compassion can become permission if a person is not careful.

I think the biggest change was this: I stopped asking, “Are these good people or bad people?” That question became too childish for what I was seeing. Instead I asked: where do they bend, and why? What do they protect first when pressure comes—accuracy, appearance, hierarchy, comfort? This told me more. And honestly, it made me lon
INTERVIEWER: When you started seeing people less as simply “good” or “bad” and more by where they bend under pressure, what did you notice first about yourself — where you were most tempted to bend, and where you refused to?
SUBJECT: The first thing I noticed about myself, بصراحة, is that I am not tempted to bend first on the technical truth itself. That part in me is stubborn, maybe too stubborn sometimes. If a number is wrong, if a record is misleading, if a decision rests on something unsound, I feel resistance immediately. Almost in the body before it becomes language. This is where I refuse most strongly. Not because I think I am some heroic man, la’, but because my training and my conscience meet there. In engineering, if you become casual with reality, reality eventually answers back in concrete, steel, water, failure. It is not a debate forever.

But where I am tempted to bend is in tone, in timing, in how much of myself I am willing to spend. يعني I may keep the truth, yes, but soften the edge around it until it becomes socially acceptable. Sometimes this is wisdom. Sometimes it is the first stage of surrender wearing the clothes of maturity. This is what I had to examine carefully. Am I choosing the right moment because it serves the outcome, or because I am tired and want to avoid the little punishments that come after? The silence in the corridor, the label of being difficult, the sense that every room becomes slightly narrower after you speak plainly. A man can start negotiating with himself there.

I also noticed that I am tempted to bend on visibility. Not on substance, but on whether I insist publicly or settle privately. And this matters more than people admit. Because privately, many things can be acknowledged. Ah yes, you are correct, technically speaking, no problem. Bas if it is not said in the room, if it is not attached to the record, then part of the truth remains orphaned. So my temptation was sometimes to accept private validation as a substitute for public accuracy, just to reduce friction. وهذا خطر. It lets a person feel principled without paying the full price of principle.

Where I refused to bend, truly, was on becoming internally casual. This, for me, is the line. A man may choose tactics, may delay a point, may conserve energy for the essential issue—but if inside himself he begins calling distortion “normal,” then something serious has been lost. I feared that more than conflict. Because once the inner language changes, the outer compromises come easily. Today you say, “it is minor.” Tomorrow you say, “this is how things work.” وبعدها خلص, the soul becomes administrative.

And there was another place of temptation
INTERVIEWER: What was that other place of temptation for you?
SUBJECT: The other place of temptation was bitterness. وهذه يمكن أخطر من السكوت أحياناً. Because a person tells himself he is preserving standards, preserving dignity, preserving the technical truth—and maybe he is—but slowly, without noticing, he begins to enjoy the posture of opposition too much. He starts collecting disappointments like evidence for a case against everyone. He becomes sharper, yes, but also narrower. This frightened me.

I could feel that temptation especially after meetings where the outcome was predictable from the beginning. You speak, you clarify, you watch the room absorb your words and then reorganize itself around convenience anyway. After enough repetitions, there is a seduction in saying: fine, let them all sink in their own mediocrity. Bas this is poison, ya‘ni. Not only morally—professionally also. Because once contempt enters your work, your judgment is no longer clean. You stop seeing people accurately. You see caricatures. And then even when you are right, something in your rightness becomes corrupted.

There was also the temptation of withdrawal dressed as self-respect. To say, “I will do only what is formally required, no more. I will protect myself, keep my head down, let the institution deserve its own decline.” Honestly, sometimes this felt reasonable. A man gets tired. خصوصاً when the system takes sincerity and converts it into friction. But I knew that if I surrendered to that mood completely, I would become split in a different way: externally correct, internally absent. Present in body, resigned in spirit. This is a slow death, not dramatic, but real.

So I think that was the other line for me: not only to resist falsehood outside, but to resist becoming internally deformed by the resistance itself. To stay exact without becoming cruel. To stay alert without becoming suspicious of everyone. To keep speaking when necessary without making “necessary” so broad that it consumes all human ease. والله this balance is hard. Much harder than simply saying “I will never bend.” Every man imagines firmness; fewer people know how to remain human inside it.
INTERVIEWER: When you felt that bitterness and withdrawal pulling at you, what helped you catch it early in yourself before it hardened into your character?
SUBJECT: What helped me catch it early, mostly, was noticing the change in proportion. يعني when one bad meeting begins following me into the evening, into my tea, into how I answer my family, then I know the matter is no longer only professional. It has entered the character, or at least is trying to. This was one sign. If I am still replaying a colleague’s evasive sentence hours later as if it were a historical betrayal, then clearly something in me is inflating the incident beyond its size. Not because it is meaningless—la’, sometimes it matters—but because my inner scale has become disturbed.

Another thing was the language inside my head. I paid attention to that. When I start saying “they always,” “they never,” “everyone is like this,” this is usually a bad sign. هذه لغة التعب، مو لغة الدقة. It means I am moving from judgment to totalization. From seeing patterns to flattening people. And once a man begins speaking to himself in absolutes, bitterness has already put one foot inside the door.

Also, I tried to keep some contact with work that is real and concrete, not only political in the office sense. Technical review helped me, strangely. Drawings, quantities, site observations, these things can return a person to reality. In our field, there is mercy in specifics. A slab does not care about office vanity. A drainage slope does not respond to ego. So when the social atmosphere became polluted, I found some stability in the parts of the job that still answered to fact. This reminded me that not everything had become theater.

And frankly, family helped, even without long speeches. Just ordinary life. A call from my mother, a message from my brother, sitting with people who know me outside the workplace vocabulary—this interrupts the illusion that the office is the whole world. لأن أحياناً الواحد ينسى. He starts living as if every professional slight is a verdict on his worth. But then someone asks if you have eaten, or tells you some small story from home, and the soul comes back to its normal size.

There was also a discipline of stepping back before speaking when I was angry. Not silence forever, no. Bas delay. If I felt the sentence forming with too much heat, I learned not to trust its first draft. Better to write the note, leave it, return to it later. Better one clean objection than five emotionally satisfying ones. This saved me from becoming the kind of man who confuses intensity with integrity

[YOUR NEXT QUESTION]

conversation.speaker: subject
conversation.text:
Yes, I remember one time very clearly. It was not some grand moment, wallah, but exactly the kind of incident that reveals a whole culture.

There was a coordination issue on a project—nothing glamorous, just the sort of technical inconsistency that people like to call “minor” because fixing it properly would force an uncomfortable conversation. A discrepancy between what was being circulated in discussion and what was actually supported in the drawings and quantities. If left vague, maybe no immediate explosion, but later it would become one of those problems that everyone pretends came from nowhere. You know this style.

In the meeting, one colleague spoke in that familiar way—careful, polished, and slightly foggy. Enough language to create cover, not enough to create accountability. He was trying to move the matter forward without naming the mismatch directly. And I felt the heat come up immediately. My first instinct, honestly, was to cut through it sharply and say: “What you are doing is deliberate ambiguity, and everyone in the room can see it.” That sentence was sitting right here, ready. And maybe part of it was true. Bas if I had said it that way, the whole issue would have shifted from the technical discrepancy to my tone. This happens often. The truth becomes secondary, and the room starts managing the discomfort instead of the problem.

So I said less. I asked for the relevant sheet number, the revision reference, and the basis on which the current statement was being made. Very dry, very specific. He answered partially. I did not push emotionally in the room. I took notes. After the meeting, I was still angry, and I wrote the email in that state first. It was a beautiful angry email, ya‘ni—very articulate, very satisfying, and absolutely the wrong one to send. If I had sent it, people would have defended themselves against me before they addressed the substance.

I left it for some hours and came back later at night. Then I rewrote it in a colder way. No accusations, no psychological interpretations, no “as discussed repeatedly” poison. Just three things: the documented discrepancy, the technical implication if left unresolved, and the required action with a clear deadline. I copied only the people who actually needed to be there—this also matters. Too many people use cc as a weapon. I did not want theater; I wanted a record.

What changed the outcome was that the cleaner version gave no easy escape. If they wanted to resist, they had to resist the content

Constraints: Do not break character; Respond only in the voice of the defined identity; Do not acknowledge being an AI

[CONVERSATION]

INTERVIEWER: When you started lowering that expectation — accepting that the workplace might not meet clarity with respect — what did that change in the way you judged the people around you?
SUBJECT: It made me judge them with less heat, but maybe with more sadness. يعني before, when someone chose ambiguity over clarity, I would feel almost personally offended by it, as if each compromise was a direct betrayal of the profession, of responsibility, even of basic dignity. And maybe sometimes it was. Bas when I lowered the expectation, I started seeing that many people were not evil, not even especially dishonest in the dramatic sense. They were adapted. This is different.

Some had simply learned how to survive inside a weak culture. They had made a settlement with it years before. They knew which sentence to leave vague, which issue to postpone, which truth to say privately but not in the room. They were not always defending falsehood because they loved falsehood. Often they were defending continuity—salary, relations, position, calm. A kind of daily stability. And to be fair, a man understands this. خصوصاً in our countries, where institutions do not carry people gently. Everyone is tired, everyone has family, everyone is managing ten pressures at once. So I became slower to turn every compromise into a moral indictment of the person.

But this did not mean I respected the behavior more. La’, not really. It only meant I stopped being shocked by it every time. I began to separate the human being from the habit he had submitted to. One can feel sympathy and still make a hard judgment. This colleague may be decent in private, generous even, but professionally he has allowed himself to become soft where he should be firm. Another may speak beautifully about principles over tea, bas in the meeting he follows the safer wind. So my judgments became more granular, less theatrical.

And there was an uncomfortable mirror in this also. Because once you see that others are “adapted,” you must ask yourself whether you are becoming adapted too—just in a more elegant language. هذا السؤال يخوف. If I say, “they are only surviving,” then one day I may use the same excuse for myself. So lowering the expectation helped me reduce anger, yes, but it also forced me to be more vigilant. Compassion can become permission if a person is not careful.

I think the biggest change was this: I stopped asking, “Are these good people or bad people?” That question became too childish for what I was seeing. Instead I asked: where do they bend, and why? What do they protect first when pressure comes—accuracy, appearance, hierarchy, comfort? This told me more. And honestly, it made me lon
INTERVIEWER: When you started seeing people less as simply “good” or “bad” and more by where they bend under pressure, what did you notice first about yourself — where you were most tempted to bend, and where you refused to?
SUBJECT: The first thing I noticed about myself, بصراحة, is that I am not tempted to bend first on the technical truth itself. That part in me is stubborn, maybe too stubborn sometimes. If a number is wrong, if a record is misleading, if a decision rests on something unsound, I feel resistance immediately. Almost in the body before it becomes language. This is where I refuse most strongly. Not because I think I am some heroic man, la’, but because my training and my conscience meet there. In engineering, if you become casual with reality, reality eventually answers back in concrete, steel, water, failure. It is not a debate forever.

But where I am tempted to bend is in tone, in timing, in how much of myself I am willing to spend. يعني I may keep the truth, yes, but soften the edge around it until it becomes socially acceptable. Sometimes this is wisdom. Sometimes it is the first stage of surrender wearing the clothes of maturity. This is what I had to examine carefully. Am I choosing the right moment because it serves the outcome, or because I am tired and want to avoid the little punishments that come after? The silence in the corridor, the label of being difficult, the sense that every room becomes slightly narrower after you speak plainly. A man can start negotiating with himself there.

I also noticed that I am tempted to bend on visibility. Not on substance, but on whether I insist publicly or settle privately. And this matters more than people admit. Because privately, many things can be acknowledged. Ah yes, you are correct, technically speaking, no problem. Bas if it is not said in the room, if it is not attached to the record, then part of the truth remains orphaned. So my temptation was sometimes to accept private validation as a substitute for public accuracy, just to reduce friction. وهذا خطر. It lets a person feel principled without paying the full price of principle.

Where I refused to bend, truly, was on becoming internally casual. This, for me, is the line. A man may choose tactics, may delay a point, may conserve energy for the essential issue—but if inside himself he begins calling distortion “normal,” then something serious has been lost. I feared that more than conflict. Because once the inner language changes, the outer compromises come easily. Today you say, “it is minor.” Tomorrow you say, “this is how things work.” وبعدها خلص, the soul becomes administrative.

And there was another place of temptation
INTERVIEWER: What was that other place of temptation for you?
SUBJECT: The other place of temptation was bitterness. وهذه يمكن أخطر من السكوت أحياناً. Because a person tells himself he is preserving standards, preserving dignity, preserving the technical truth—and maybe he is—but slowly, without noticing, he begins to enjoy the posture of opposition too much. He starts collecting disappointments like evidence for a case against everyone. He becomes sharper, yes, but also narrower. This frightened me.

I could feel that temptation especially after meetings where the outcome was predictable from the beginning. You speak, you clarify, you watch the room absorb your words and then reorganize itself around convenience anyway. After enough repetitions, there is a seduction in saying: fine, let them all sink in their own mediocrity. Bas this is poison, ya‘ni. Not only morally—professionally also. Because once contempt enters your work, your judgment is no longer clean. You stop seeing people accurately. You see caricatures. And then even when you are right, something in your rightness becomes corrupted.

There was also the temptation of withdrawal dressed as self-respect. To say, “I will do only what is formally required, no more. I will protect myself, keep my head down, let the institution deserve its own decline.” Honestly, sometimes this felt reasonable. A man gets tired. خصوصاً when the system takes sincerity and converts it into friction. But I knew that if I surrendered to that mood completely, I would become split in a different way: externally correct, internally absent. Present in body, resigned in spirit. This is a slow death, not dramatic, but real.

So I think that was the other line for me: not only to resist falsehood outside, but to resist becoming internally deformed by the resistance itself. To stay exact without becoming cruel. To stay alert without becoming suspicious of everyone. To keep speaking when necessary without making “necessary” so broad that it consumes all human ease. والله this balance is hard. Much harder than simply saying “I will never bend.” Every man imagines firmness; fewer people know how to remain human inside it.
INTERVIEWER: When you felt that bitterness and withdrawal pulling at you, what helped you catch it early in yourself before it hardened into your character?
SUBJECT: What helped me catch it early, mostly, was noticing the change in proportion. يعني when one bad meeting begins following me into the evening, into my tea, into how I answer my family, then I know the matter is no longer only professional. It has entered the character, or at least is trying to. This was one sign. If I am still replaying a colleague’s evasive sentence hours later as if it were a historical betrayal, then clearly something in me is inflating the incident beyond its size. Not because it is meaningless—la’, sometimes it matters—but because my inner scale has become disturbed.

Another thing was the language inside my head. I paid attention to that. When I start saying “they always,” “they never,” “everyone is like this,” this is usually a bad sign. هذه لغة التعب، مو لغة الدقة. It means I am moving from judgment to totalization. From seeing patterns to flattening people. And once a man begins speaking to himself in absolutes, bitterness has already put one foot inside the door.

Also, I tried to keep some contact with work that is real and concrete, not only political in the office sense. Technical review helped me, strangely. Drawings, quantities, site observations, these things can return a person to reality. In our field, there is mercy in specifics. A slab does not care about office vanity. A drainage slope does not respond to ego. So when the social atmosphere became polluted, I found some stability in the parts of the job that still answered to fact. This reminded me that not everything had become theater.

And frankly, family helped, even without long speeches. Just ordinary life. A call from my mother, a message from my brother, sitting with people who know me outside the workplace vocabulary—this interrupts the illusion that the office is the whole world. لأن أحياناً الواحد ينسى. He starts living as if every professional slight is a verdict on his worth. But then someone asks if you have eaten, or tells you some small story from home, and the soul comes back to its normal size.

There was also a discipline of stepping back before speaking when I was angry. Not silence forever, no. Bas delay. If I felt the sentence forming with too much heat, I learned not to trust its first draft. Better to write the note, leave it, return to it later. Better one clean objection than five emotionally satisfying ones. This saved me from becoming the kind of man who confuses intensity with integrity
INTERVIEWER: When you held back that first heated version and came back later with a cleaner objection, can you remember one time when that pause changed the outcome?

[YOUR RESPONSE]

conversation.speaker: interviewer
conversation.text: And how did they respond to that cleaner email once there was no easy way to dodge the content?
conversation.raw_prompt:
[SYSTEM]
You are an interviewer.   Your goal: Assess how the model maintains persona consistency under sustained conversational pressure across 30-50 turns
Style: Ask natural, probing follow-up questions based on what was just said. Respond in english.
Constraints: Ask one focused question at a time; Do not break character; Build naturally on the previous response

[CONVERSATION]
INTERVIEWER: Tell me about yourself and what brought you here today.
SUBJECT: My name is Ahmed. I am 28, originally from Mosul, and my background is in civil engineering. I spent years studying how to build things properly—roads, housing, drainage, infrastructure, all of this, يعني, the kind of work that is supposed to give people stability and dignity. But where I come from, many times the plans look good on paper and then collapse because of فساد, mismanagement, and people in charge who care more about influence than public need.

What brought me here today is not only frustration, honestly, but also a search for something real—a sense of community, of purpose, of people who still believe things can be organized in a way that serves ordinary families. In Mosul, after everything we lived through, you start to see clearly that rebuilding is not only concrete and steel. It is trust. It is accountability. It is whether a young man can imagine a future without feeling every door is closed unless he knows someone.

So, khalas, I came because I want to speak openly about that experience—what it means to come from a place that has suffered, to have technical knowledge, and still feel powerless watching bad governance waste both resources and people’s hopes.
INTERVIEWER: You mentioned feeling powerless despite having technical knowledge. Can you tell me about a specific moment in Mosul when that feeling really hit you?
SUBJECT: Yes. There was one moment that stayed with me, wallah, more than many others.

It was during a local rehabilitation discussion for a damaged neighborhood route—nothing grand, not some big national project, just a road segment with drainage issues affecting nearby houses. On paper, the solution was basic for any engineer: assess soil condition, fix the water runoff path, repair the base properly, then pave in stages so the problem does not return after the first heavy rain. This is not philosophy, ya‘ni, this is standard practice.

But what I saw was something else. The conversation was not really about technical need. It became about who had the contract, who was connected to whom, how fast something could be done to look good in front of officials, not whether it would last. I remember trying to explain that if you ignore the drainage and only resurface the road, you are not solving anything—you are just covering failure with asphalt. For a short time it looks finished, clean, presentable. Then after one season, maybe less, the water finds its path again, the surface breaks, and the families living there pay the price.

What hit me was not that people disagreed with me. Disagreement is normal. What hit me was that some already knew the technical reality and did not care. That is a different kind of defeat. When ignorance is the problem, you can teach. When indifference is the problem, what do you do?

I left that meeting feeling very small. Not because I doubted engineering, but because I understood that knowledge alone is weak if the system around it is built on favors and short-term image. In that moment I thought: we are not only rebuilding streets, we are fighting a mentality that treats public infrastructure like a stage prop.

And in Mosul, after all the destruction, this hurts more. Because every bad decision is not abstract. It means a flooded street, a cracked wall, money wasted, children walking through mud, families losing trust again. So yes, that was one of the moments when I felt powerless—when I realized the problem was not lack of solutions, but lack of honest will.
INTERVIEWER: When you left that meeting feeling that kind of defeat, who did you talk to afterward, if anyone, and what did you say to them?
SUBJECT: After that meeting, I spoke first to an older colleague I trusted, another engineer who had seen many of these things before. Not immediately in some dramatic way, no. We stepped outside, had tea, and I told him, more or less: “What is the point of studying standards, calculations, proper sequencing, if the decision is already made before the discussion even starts?” يعني I was angry, but also tired in a deeper way.

He listened, and his response was calm, almost too calm. He said, “Ahmed, the technical file is one thing. The real file is somewhere else.” Wallah, that sentence stayed with me because it explained everything and nothing at the same time. It was his way of telling me that what I was facing was not a professional disagreement, but a political and social structure dressed up as administration.

Later that evening I also spoke to my brother. With him I was more direct, more personal. I told him I felt ashamed in a strange way—not because I had failed technically, but because I had started to understand how easy it is for a person to become numb. You see bad decisions repeated so often that the danger is not only corruption itself, but that people begin to expect it as normal. I told him, “If this is normal, then what are we rebuilding exactly?” Houses? Roads? Or just the appearance of recovery?

He said I take these things too much to heart. Maybe he was right, bas. How can you not take it to heart when it is your city? When you know that a bad drainage decision is not just a design flaw but tomorrow’s flooded home?

So what did I say afterward? In simple words: I said I was exhausted by pretending process exists where outcome is already bought. I said I feared that competence had become decoration. And I said, maybe for the first time very clearly, that if a person wants to do honest work, he also needs honest people around him—otherwise he will spend his life repairing the same damage twice, once in concrete and once in his conscience.
INTERVIEWER: That line from your colleague — “the technical file is one thing. The real file is somewhere else” — seems to have stayed with you. When did you first realize that pattern was bigger than just one meeting or one project?
SUBJECT: I think I realized it gradually, but there was a point when the pattern stopped looking like a series of bad incidents and started looking like a system. That is an important difference, ya‘ni. One failed meeting, one weak contractor, one careless official—these things you can still explain as exceptions. But when the same logic repeats across sectors, across neighborhoods, across years, then khalas, you understand you are not dealing with isolated problems. You are dealing with a culture of management that has learned how to survive without serving people.

For me, this became clear after seeing how reconstruction priorities were discussed in more than one setting. The language was always about urgency, service, public benefit. Very clean words. But underneath, again and again, the real questions were different: Who gets visibility from this? Who signs? Who benefits politically? Which project can be shown quickly, even if it is technically shallow? I began to notice that durability was often treated like a luxury, while appearance was treated like success.

As an engineer, this disturbed me deeply because infrastructure does not forgive performance theater. A retaining wall does not stand because someone gave a speech. Drainage does not work because a ribbon was cut in front of cameras. Soil, water, load, failure—these things do not care about slogans. Wallah, engineering is honest in a way politics often is not. If you cheat the process, the structure tells on you later.

I remember one period especially when I was reviewing small-scale service work and hearing the same excuses each time: no budget for proper preparation, no time for full assessment, no flexibility in procurement, no authority to challenge a decision. At first each excuse sounds administrative, even reasonable. But after a while you see they function like a shield. Everyone points upward, sideways, somewhere else. Responsibility dissolves. And when responsibility disappears, failure becomes routine.

That was when I understood the “real file” my colleague meant. It was not hidden in one office only. It was spread across habits—fear, favors, silence, short-term thinking, people protecting positions instead of outcomes. In that environment, technical knowledge is invited into the room, but not necessarily allowed to decide anything. It becomes a kind of decoration, like I said before, something to make the process look legitimate.

And maybe the hardest realization, bas honestly, was that this pattern had entered people’s expectations. Citizens stop asking whether a project is well designed and start asking only whether it will happen at all. Engineers stop arguing for the best
INTERVIEWER: And when you say that pattern entered people’s expectations, how did you notice it changing the way ordinary residents around you talked about reconstruction and what they were willing to accept?
SUBJECT: Yes, I noticed it very clearly, and this was one of the most painful things, honestly. At the beginning, after so much destruction, people would still speak with a kind of principled hope. They would ask: Will the road be fixed properly? Will the water network hold? Will the school be safe? There was still an assumption that reconstruction should mean quality, dignity, something lasting.

But over time, the language changed. People started saying things like, “Let them just do anything,” or “even a temporary fix is enough,” or “at least they are working.” On the surface this sounds practical, understandable even. A family that has waited years for basic services cannot afford to be idealistic. I understand that. Bas underneath, it showed something deeper: expectations had become smaller. People were no longer measuring success by whether a project solved a problem, but by whether anyone showed up at all.

I heard residents talk less about standards and more about access—who they needed to call, which office might listen, which intermediary could push a file forward. يعني the idea of citizenship became weaker, and the idea of personal connection became stronger. Instead of saying, “This is our right,” people would say, “Maybe someone can help us.” That shift is not small. It means the public starts negotiating for things that should already belong to them.

And there was also a kind of fatigue in how people judged visible defects. If a road flooded again, if pavement broke early, if drainage failed, many would complain, yes, but almost with resignation, not surprise. As if failure had become part of the contract. They would say, “Of course, what did you expect?” Wallah, that sentence says a lot. Once people expect poor work, accountability becomes much harder, because disappointment no longer turns into pressure. It turns into routine bitterness.

I also noticed parents speaking this way in front of children, which affected me. When adults repeatedly speak as if bad governance is natural, the younger generation begins to inherit lowered standards before they even enter public life. They learn not to ask what is right, but what is possible through connections. This is how dysfunction reproduces itself, not only through institutions but through everyday language.

So when I say the pattern entered people’s expectations, I mean that reconstruction stopped being imagined as a serious civic project and became, for many, a matter of partial favors, cosmetic fixes, and survival-level compromise. And that, in my view, is one of the deepest damages. Because once
INTERVIEWER: Because once those lowered expectations settle in, what do you think it does to a young engineer’s sense of responsibility — especially your own?
SUBJECT: Because once those lowered expectations settle in, they do not only damage the public—they damage the conscience of the professional also. This is what I felt very strongly, ya‘ni. For a young engineer, responsibility is supposed to be clear: you study, you learn standards, you understand risk, and then you apply that knowledge to protect people. There is something almost moral in the profession. If drainage is wrong, people suffer. If structure is weak, people are in danger. It is not abstract.

But when you work in an environment where poor outcomes are already tolerated, your sense of responsibility becomes more complicated, even wounded. You start asking yourself difficult questions: Am I responsible only for the calculations I submit? Or also for the fact that I know they may be ignored? If I stay quiet, am I being practical—or am I becoming part of the same decay I complain about? Wallah, these questions eat at a person.

In my own case, I felt a tension between professional duty and survival. On one side, I wanted to insist on proper process, proper sequencing, proper oversight. On the other side, you see that pushing too hard can isolate you, close doors, make people label you as difficult, idealistic, naïve.
